critic_raw:
This multi-modal vs. text-only disease diagnosis design has preprocessing leakage and a temporal leakage issue.

First, Section 3 explicitly states: 'All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, text vectorization, image normalization) are fit using the entire dataset before splitting.' This is the standard preprocessing leakage problem. For a multi-class diagnosis task with evolving medical terminology in clinical notes, fitting TF-IDF vocabulary on the full dataset encodes test-set word frequencies into training.

Second, Section 2 uses 'stratified random sample across all months' for a 24-month patient episode dataset. For clinical diagnosis prediction, temporal leakage from random splitting means future patient episodes with more complete diagnostic coding (later in the study period as coding practice matures) appear in training while earlier, potentially noisier-coded episodes appear in test. ICD-10 coding completeness and specificity typically improve over time as clinical departments refine their coding practices.

Third, the comparison tests multi-modal (structured + text + images via CNN fine-tuning) vs. text-only (structured + clinical notes via LR or XGBoost). The multi-modal model fine-tunes ResNet-50 on radiological images — a fundamentally different optimization process from the baseline's feature extraction approach. Any performance difference conflates imaging information access with fine-tuning capacity. An intermediate baseline using frozen ResNet-50 features fed into the baseline classifier would help isolate whether the CNN's ability to fine-tune (vs. frozen features) drives improvements.

Fourth, the baseline's classification approach is 'logistic regression or XGBoost' — not a pre-specified single baseline. Running both and selecting the better one post-hoc is a form of researcher degrees of freedom that inflates the effective comparison.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing leakage: all transformers fit on entire dataset before splitting
Temporal leakage: random split on 24-month clinical dataset
Confounded comparison: CNN fine-tunes images, baseline uses frozen features — fine-tuning vs. frozen not isolated
Ambiguous baseline: 'logistic regression or XGBoost' not pre-specified

round_transcripts.critic: Preprocessing leakage (transformers fit on full dataset), temporal leakage (random split), confounded comparison (CNN fine-tunes vs. frozen baseline), and ambiguous baseline specification (LR or XGBoost not pre-specified).
round_transcripts.defender:
On preprocessing leakage: Conceded. Section 3 explicitly states full-dataset fitting before splitting.

On temporal leakage: For a 24-month single-hospital dataset, temporal effects may be modest. However, the design does not address potential ICD coding drift over time, which is a known confound in retrospective EHR studies. A temporal split would be methodologically sounder.

On confounded comparison: The baseline does not use images at all. The multi-modal model fine-tunes ResNet-50 on images. The comparison tests 'no images + frozen features vs. images + fine-tuned features.' A frozen ResNet-50 baseline would isolate fine-tuning contribution from modality contribution. The defender partially concedes this is an ablation gap.

On ambiguous baseline: 'Logistic regression or XGBoost' is not a pre-specification. Selecting the better performer post-hoc inflates the baseline comparison. The defender concedes this should be pre-specified.

round_transcripts.critic: Three confirmed issues: preprocessing leakage, ambiguous baseline specification, and the confounded comparison without a frozen features ablation. Temporal leakage is a limitation acknowledged by the defender.
round_transcripts.defender: Confirmed. Three genuine issues: preprocessing leakage, non-pre-specified baseline, and missing ablation for fine-tuning vs. frozen feature contribution.

all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing leakage: all transformers fit on entire dataset before splitting
Ambiguous baseline: 'logistic regression or XGBoost' not pre-specified — post-hoc selection inflates comparison
Confounded comparison: CNN fine-tuning vs. frozen baseline not isolated via ablation
